title: Tetralogy of Fallot - Hypercyanotic Spell
chief_complaint: My baby suddenly turned blue, became very irritable, and then went limp. He's been crying inconsolably. This lasted about 2 minutes then he recovered. He has a known heart condition that hasn't been repaired yet.

physical_exam.General: Cyanotic infant. Clubbing of fingers and toes.
physical_exam.Cardiac: Harsh systolic ejection murmur at left upper sternal border (pulmonic stenosis). Single S2 (absent pulmonic component). RV heave.

hidden_data.cxr: Boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot) from RVH and upturned apex. Decreased pulmonary vascular markings. Right aortic arch (25% of TOF).
hidden_data.echo: Large malalignment VSD. Overriding aorta (50%). RVOT obstruction (infundibular + valvular pulmonic stenosis). RVH. Confirms TOF.
hidden_data.ecg: Right axis deviation. RVH (tall R waves in V1-V2).
hidden_data.cbc: Hb 18.5, Hct 58% (secondary polycythemia from chronic hypoxia)

correct_diagnosis: Tetralogy of Fallot with Hypercyanotic (Tet) Spell
key_learning_points: TOF = most common cyanotic congenital heart disease. Four features: VSD, Overriding aorta, Pulmonic stenosis (RVOT obstruction), RVH. Degree of RVOT obstruction determines severity
Tet spell = acute hypoxia from increased RVOT obstruction (infundibular spasm). Triggers: Crying, feeding, defecation, waking from sleep. Peak incidence 2-4 months
Spell physiology: RVOT spasm → R→L shunt via VSD increases → deoxygenated blood enters systemic circulation → severe cyanosis, metabolic acidosis. Can lead to syncope, seizures, death

Treatment of spell: Knee-chest position (increases SVR → decreases R→L shunt), Oxygen, Morphine 0.1mg/kg IM/IV (sedation, decreases infundibular spasm), Phenylephrine 5-20mcg/kg IV (increases SVR), IV fluids, NaHCO3 for acidosis. Propranolol 0.01mg/kg IV for refractory (relaxes infundibulum)

Complete surgical repair: Elective at 3-6 months. Indications for early repair: Severe cyanosis, Tet spells, failure to thrive. Complications post-repair: Pulmonary regurgitation (most common, may need PVR later), RVOT aneurysm, arrhythmias, sudden death risk
